variables.content: The DMC-calculated quasiparticle effective mass exhibits opposite trends with density for the two spin polarizations studied: in the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG $m^{*}$ increases with increasing $r_{s}$ (decreasing density), producing a significant enhancement at $r_{s}=5$ and $r_{s}=10$, whereas in the fully ferromagnetic (fully spin-polarized) 2D HEG $m^{*}$ decreases as density is lowered; these calculated trends are quantitatively consistent with the experimental observations of Tan et al. for paramagnetic systems and of Padmanabhan et al. for ferromagnetic or partially spin-polarized systems.

For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10, let \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denote the quasiparticle effective mass extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting the finite-size DMC effective masses to the single-power-law form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) and adopting the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolation. The proposition is: the extrapolated numerical values \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\) indicate that the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass of the clean continuum-model paramagnetic 2D HEG remains close to unity across this \\(r_{s}\\) range and that there is no significant enhancement of \\(m^{*}\\) at low density for this physical system class.

Let the experimental ferromagnetic effective-mass measurements of Padmanabhan et al. have been obtained in the presence of an external magnetic field whose component normal to the two-dimensional electron layer was relatively small; the proposition is that comparing zero-field theoretical DMC calculations of a fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG with those experimental ferromagnetic measurements is valid because the small normal component of the experimental magnetic field does not materially alter the single-particle band and effective-mass behavior relevant to the comparison, so that the theoretical idealization and the experimental conditions are sufficiently compatible for the stated quantitative agreement to be meaningful.

Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.

The extrapolated thermodynamic-limit effective masses exhibit distinct density dependences depending on spin polarization: for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10 the extrapolated effective mass remains close to unity (\\(m^{*}\\approx1\\)), indicating no significant interaction-induced mass enhancement at these densities for the clean continuum 2D HEG model considered; by contrast, for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG the extrapolated effective mass decreases as the density is lowered, for example taking the values \\(0.85(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=1\\), \\(0.74(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=5\\), and \\(0.70(3)\\) at \\(r_{s}=10\\), a trend that is qualitatively consistent with experimental measurements on spin-polarized two-dimensional systems reported in the literature (e.g., Padmanabhan et al.).

The DMC effective masses show opposite trends with increasing $r_s$ (decreasing density) for the two spin polarizations studied: for the paramagnetic 2D HEG $m^{*}$ increases with increasing interaction strength over the range examined (from approximately $0.95$ at $r_s=1$ to approximately $1.34$ at $r_s=10$), whereas for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG $m^{*}$ decreases with increasing $r_s$ (from approximately $0.86$ at $r_s=1$ to approximately $0.61$ at $r_s=10$). These calculated trends are consistent with experimental measurements that report an increase of the paramagnetic effective mass as density is lowered (Tan et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 94, 016405 (2005)) and a decrease of the effective mass in ferromagnetic or partially spin-polarized samples as density is lowered (Padmanabhan et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 101, 026402 (2008) and related work).

The occupied single-particle energy band $\mathcal{E}(k)$ of the interacting two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) was computed using the diffusion quantum Monte Carlo (DMC) method by taking total-energy differences on adding or removing a single electron in a plane-wave orbital $\exp(i\mathbf{k}\!\cdot\!\mathbf{r})$; the resulting discrete DMC band values for the occupied states were described by a least-squares fit to the quartic form $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_{0}+\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\alpha_{4}k^{4}$, where $\alpha_{0}$, $\alpha_{2}$, and $\alpha_{4}$ are fitted coefficients.  These DMC calculations were performed for both the paramagnetic (unpolarized) and the ferromagnetic (fully spin-polarized) 2D HEGs at density-parameter values $r_{s}=1$, $5$, and $10$ (atomic units, where lengths are given in Bohr radii).  The fitted quartic shows clear nonquadratic behavior: for the paramagnetic fluid $\alpha_{4}>0$ at $r_{s}=5$ and $r_{s}=10$, for the ferromagnetic fluid $\alpha_{4}<0$ at all three densities studied, and for the paramagnetic fluid at $r_{s}=1$ the fitted DMC band is very close to the free-electron dispersion $\mathcal{E}(k)=k^{2}/2$ (all energies in Hartree atomic units).

The zero-time-step, twist-averaged diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) ground-state energies per electron for the infinite paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) at the reported density parameters are: $E(r_{s}=1)= -0.2104(6)$ a.u. per electron, $E(r_{s}=5)= -0.14963(3)$ a.u. per electron, and $E(r_{s}=10)= -0.085399(6)$ a.u. per electron; here $r_{s}$ is the radius (in Hartree atomic units) of the circle containing one electron on average, energies are given in Hartree atomic units, the DMC calculations used Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions to define the fixed-node constraint, twist averaging where indicated to reduce single-particle finite-size effects, and the reported values have been extrapolated to infinite system size.

Let the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG be represented theoretically by the continuum-model DMC calculations and experimentally by high-quality semiconductor two-dimensional systems in which spin polarization can be induced (for example by applying an in-plane magnetic field). The proposition is: the suppression of the quasiparticle effective mass with decreasing density observed in the DMC-extrapolated ferromagnetic results (numerically \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\)) corresponds qualitatively to the trend measured in experiments cited in the literature (for example Padmanabhan et al.), and this qualitative agreement is not invalidated by differences in sample specifics, partial polarization, disorder, or experimental measurement methodology.

For the ideal (strictly two-dimensional, zero-width, disorder-free) paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas, the extrapolated thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass remains close to unity across the studied density parameters r_s=1, 5, and 10, and the high-precision DMC extrapolations reported here show no evidence for a strong enhancement of m^* at low density in this ideal system.
